items.title: investingLive Asia-Pacific FX news wrap: Trump reviewing ceasefire extension, markets wait
items.summary: Dollar faces renewed strength if US-Iran talks fail, MUFG warns Bank of England Bailey, Mann and Greene due to speak at conferences: rates, crypto NZ business confidence bounces in May but Middle East cost squeeze persists RBNZ's Breman signals faster and larger rate hikes than previously flagged PBOC sets USD/ CNY reference rate for today at 6.8176 (vs. estimate at 6.7685) Katayama warns on yen volatility as intervention data looms at 1900 JST More Japan data: April retail sales and Manufacturing output both rise fast than expected Tokyo core CPI misses forecasts in May, complicating case for BOJ June rate hike Dell earnings explained: why the stock exploded after Q1 FY27 results RBNZ's Silk flags near-term inflation pressure and rate hikes in coming meetings Tokyo May CPI data: Headline, core, and core-core all rise more slowly than in April Yen edges back toward 160 as traders await Japan intervention data due Friday DELL up 30% in after hours trading - here's why (massive contract with US military) US Vice Pres Vance says Trump not yet ready to endorse Iran agreement NZ consumer confidence lifts off lows but remains well below January peak US officials say there have been no US aircraft shot down, contradicting Iranian claims Recap: US-Iran ceasefire extension agreed but unconfirmed, markets whipsaw on war signals investingLive Americas FX news wrap 28 May: Softer PCE pressures dollar and yields Iran state TV says they destroyed a US aircraft. No US comment at this stage. Trump is speaking, says the US holds all the cards, Iran has been defeated 'militarily' The major US stock indices close at record levels At a glance: US-Iran ceasefire extension tentatively agreed but awaiting Trump sign-off; nuclear language sticking points remain; Iran's claim of shooting down a US aircraft near Bushehr denied by US authorities Tokyo core CPI missed across all measures in May, extending a six-month slowdown; core ex-food and energy at 1.6% vs 1.9% forecast; Japanese factory output surprised to the upside in April RBNZ Governor Anna Breman signalled rates will rise sooner and by more than previously indicated Russian drone struck a residential building in Galați, Romania; NATO response expected to be diplomatic rather than kinetic Dell shares surged 25%+ after blowout first-quarter earnings; Pentagon's $9.7 billion software contract an additional driver SpaceX targeting $1.8 trillion IPO valuation; Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket exploded during a hot-fire test Japan MOF intervention data due 1000 GMT / 0600 Eastern covering April 28 to May 27 A tentative agreement to extend the US-Iran ceasefire for 60 days lifted risk sentiment through the session, but the deal remained unsigned and fragile. Vice President JD Vance said President Trump had yet to endorse the memorandum of understanding, with negotiations still grinding over language on Iran's nuclear programme, specifically the disposition of Tehran's highly enriched uranium stockpile and its future enrichment rights. Vance said he believed Iran was negotiating in good faith but could not guarantee an agreement would be reached. Iranian state television claimed a US aircraft had been destroyed near Bushehr, a report US authorities flatly denied. In Japan, Tokyo's key inflation gauges missed forecasts across the board in May. Core CPI, excluding fresh food, rose 1.3% year-on-year against a 1.5% forecast, marking a sixth consecutive month of slowdown. The core-core index, stripping out both fresh food and energy, came in at 1.6% against an expected 1.9%. Government subsidies on utilities and tuition were the primary drag, with analysts expecting inflation to re-accelerate as energy and import price pressures build. Offsetting the soft inflation data, separate figures showed Japanese factory output rose 0.8% in April, confounding forecasts for a 0.9% decline, with manufacturers projecting a further 5.1% rise in May. The soft data complicates the BOJ's messaging ahead of its June policy meeting, where overnight index swaps had been pricing around an 80% probability of a hike to 1% from the current 0.75%. With subsidies distorting the headline reads, the BOJ's case for June rests heavily on the expectation that inflation will re-accelerate once those effects drop out. Japan's TOPIX moved to a record high. New Zealand dominated the central bank narrative. RBNZ Governor Anna Breman, whose casting vote broke a 3-3 split on the monetary policy committee to keep the OCR on hold at 2.25% this week, made clear the pause is temporary. Breman said the OCR is likely to increase sooner and by more than previously signalled, with the bank focused on returning inflation to target while managing economic volatility. Assistant Governor Karen Silk had earlier reinforced the hawkish message, saying the bank does not need to wait for a quarterly CPI print to act and that the bias is toward rate increases at coming meetings. The New Zealand dollar firmed on the guidance. Currencies otherwise consolidated against a broadly flat US dollar. Traders nudged away from the greenback on ceasefire optimism but stopped well short of a full risk-on move, with the deal's unsigned status keeping positioning cautious. A Russian one-way attack drone entered Romanian airspace during a strike on Ukraine and struck a residential apartment block in the eastern city of Galați, injuring several people. Romania's defence ministry confirmed the incident and scrambled F-16s. NATO's response is expected to be diplomatic, likely involving the summoning of the Russian ambassador and increased economic pressure rather than any kinetic reply. Dell shares surged more than 25% after the company reported its strongest earnings since returning to public markets in 2018. Revenue hit a record $43.8 billion, up 88% year-on-year, while diluted EPS rose 282% to $5.24. The Pentagon's award of a five-year, $9.7 billion software purchasing agreement to Dell Federal Systems added further impetus, reinforcing the market's view of Dell as a major AI infrastructure supplier rather than a traditional hardware company. Separately, SpaceX is targeting a valuation of at least $1.8 trillion in its forthcoming IPO, while Jeff Bezos' Blue Origin reported an anomaly during a hot-fire test of its New Glenn rocket, which exploded in footage circulated on social media. Japan's Ministry of Finance intervention data covering the period from April 28 to May 27 is due at 1000 GMT, 0600 Eastern. Bloomberg analysis of BOJ accounts had pointed to up to ¥10 trillion deployed in late April and through Golden Week, and the official figure will set the tone for yen trading heading into the weekend. This article was written by Eamonn Sheridan at investinglive.com.
items.summaryZh: 三菱日联金融集团警告：若美伊谈判破裂，美元将重拾涨势 英格兰银行行长贝利、曼恩和格林将出席会议并发表演讲：利率、加密货币 新西兰5月商业信心回升，但中东成本压力持续 新西兰联储的布雷曼暗示加息步伐将快于此前预期，幅度也将更大 中国人民银行将今日美元兑人民币中间价定为6.8176（市场预期为6.7685） 片山警告日元波动性，干预数据将于日本时间19:00公布 更多日本数据：4月零售额和制造业产出增速均超预期 东京5月核心CPI未达预期，为日本央行6月加息增添变数 戴尔财报解析：为何2027财年第一季度业绩公布后股价暴涨 新西兰联储的Silk指出短期通胀压力及未来会议将加息 东京5月CPI数据： 总体、核心及核心-核心通胀率涨幅均低于4月 日元汇率小幅回升至160关口附近，交易员等待周五公布的日本干预数据 戴尔盘后交易大涨30%——原因在此（与美军签订巨额合同） 美国副总统万斯表示特朗普尚未准备好支持伊朗协议 新西兰消费者信心指数脱离低位但仍远低于1月峰值 美国官员称尚未有美国 飞机被击落，与伊朗说法相矛盾 回顾：美伊停火延期已达成协议但尚未得到证实，市场因战争信号剧烈震荡 investingLive 美洲外汇新闻综述 5月28日：个人消费支出（PCE）数据走软，美元及收益率承压 伊朗国家电视台称已击落一架美军飞机。美国目前未作评论。特朗普正在发表讲话，称美国掌握主动权，伊朗已在“军事上”被击败。美国主要股指收于历史高位。简讯：美伊停火延期已初步达成协议，但尚待特朗普批准；核问题措辞仍存分歧； 美国当局否认伊朗关于在布什尔附近击落一架美军飞机的说法；5月东京核心CPI各项指标均未达预期，延续了六个月的放缓态势；剔除食品和能源的核心CPI为1.6%，低于1.9%的预期； 日本4月工业产出数据意外向好 新西兰央行行长安娜·布雷曼暗示加息将早于预期且幅度更大 俄罗斯无人机袭击罗马尼亚加拉茨市一栋居民楼；预计北约将采取外交手段而非军事行动 戴尔因第一季度财报远超预期股价暴涨逾25%；五角大楼97亿美元软件合同成为额外催化剂 SpaceX目标 g 1.8万亿美元的IPO估值；蓝色起源的“新格伦”火箭在热火测试中爆炸 日本财务省干预数据将于格林尼治时间10:00 / 东部时间06:00发布，涵盖4月28日至5月27日 关于将美伊停火协议延长60天的初步协议提振了全天的风险偏好，但该协议尚未签署且仍显脆弱。副总统JD·万斯表示，特朗普总统尚未批准该谅解备忘录，双方仍在就伊朗核计划的措辞进行艰难谈判，特别是关于德黑兰高浓缩铀库存的处置及其未来的浓缩权利。万斯表示，他相信伊朗是在真诚地进行谈判，但无法保证能达成协议。伊朗国家电视台声称一架美国飞机在布什尔附近被击落，美国当局对此予以坚决否认。在日本，东京5月份的主要通胀指标全面低于预期。剔除生鲜食品的核心CPI同比上涨1.3%，低于1.5%的预期，这标志着通胀增速连续第六个月放缓。剔除生鲜食品和能源的“核心中的核心”指数为1.6%，低于1.9%的预期。 公用事业和学费方面的政府补贴是通胀放缓的主要拖累因素，分析师预计，随着能源和进口价格压力的加剧，通胀将再次加速。与疲软的通胀数据形成对比的是，另一组数据显示，日本4月份工业产出增长0.8%，打破了此前预计将下降0.9%的预期，制造商预计5月份将进一步增长5.1%。这些疲软的数据使日本央行在6月政策会议前的表态变得复杂。此前隔夜指数掉期市场曾将6月加息概率定价在80%左右，预计政策利率将从目前的0.75%上调至1%。由于补贴扭曲了整体通胀数据，日本央行6月加息的依据主要取决于市场预期：一旦补贴效应消退，通胀将重新加速。日本东证指数（TOPIX）创下历史新高。新西兰央行成为市场关注的焦点。新西兰央行行长安娜·布雷曼（Anna Breman）本周以关键一票打破货币政策委员会3比3的僵局，将官方现金利率（OCR）维持在2.25%不变，她明确表示此次暂停加息仅是暂时的。布雷曼表示，官方现金利率可能比此前预示的更早、幅度更大地上调，央行将致力于在管理经济波动的同时，使通胀回归目标水平。 副行长卡伦·西尔克此前重申了鹰派立场，表示央行无需等待季度CPI数据出炉即可采取行动，且在即将召开的会议上倾向于加息。受此指引影响，新西兰元走强。其他货币则在美元整体持平的背景下维持盘整。受停火乐观情绪推动，交易员小幅抛售美元，但距离全面风险偏好回升尚有距离，因协议尚未签署，市场持仓仍保持谨慎。在对乌克兰发动袭击期间，一架俄罗斯单向攻击无人机侵入罗马尼亚领空，并击中东部城市加拉茨的一栋居民公寓楼，造成数人受伤。罗马尼亚国防部证实了这一事件，并紧急出动F-16战机。预计北约将采取外交手段应对，可能包括召见俄罗斯大使及加大经济施压，而非采取任何军事反击。戴尔公司公布了自2018年重返公开市场以来的最强劲财报后，其股价飙升逾25%。营收达到创纪录的438亿美元，同比增长88%，而摊薄后每股收益上涨282%至5.24美元。 五角大楼与戴尔联邦系统公司签订了一份为期五年、价值97亿美元的软件采购协议，这进一步提振了市场信心，强化了市场对戴尔作为主要人工智能基础设施供应商而非传统硬件公司的看法。此外，SpaceX计划在即将进行的首次公开募股（IPO）中寻求至少1.8万亿美元的估值，而杰夫·贝索斯旗下的蓝色起源公司则报告称，其“新格伦”火箭在热火测试期间出现异常，社交媒体上流传的视频显示该火箭发生了爆炸。日本财务省将于格林尼治时间10:00（美国东部时间06:00）公布4月28日至5月27日期间的外汇干预数据。彭博社对日本央行账户的分析显示，4月下旬至黄金周期间日本央行动用了高达10万亿日元的资金，官方数据将决定周末前日元交易的走势。本文由investinglive.com的伊蒙·谢里丹撰写。
items.summaryEn: Dollar faces renewed strength if US-Iran talks fail, MUFG warns Bank of England Bailey, Mann and Greene due to speak at conferences: rates, crypto NZ business confidence bounces in May but Middle East cost squeeze persists RBNZ's Breman signals faster and larger rate hikes than previously flagged PBOC sets USD/ CNY reference rate for today at 6.8176 (vs. estimate at 6.7685) Katayama warns on yen volatility as intervention data looms at 1900 JST More Japan data: April retail sales and Manufacturing output both rise fast than expected Tokyo core CPI misses forecasts in May, complicating case for BOJ June rate hike Dell earnings explained: why the stock exploded after Q1 FY27 results RBNZ's Silk flags near-term inflation pressure and rate hikes in coming meetings Tokyo May CPI data: Headline, core, and core-core all rise more slowly than in April Yen edges back toward 160 as traders await Japan intervention data due Friday DELL up 30% in after hours trading - here's why (massive contract with US military) US Vice Pres Vance says Trump not yet ready to endorse Iran agreement NZ consumer confidence lifts off lows but remains well below January peak US officials say there have been no US aircraft shot down, contradicting Iranian claims Recap: US-Iran ceasefire extension agreed but unconfirmed, markets whipsaw on war signals investingLive Americas FX news wrap 28 May: Softer PCE pressures dollar and yields Iran state TV says they destroyed a US aircraft. No US comment at this stage. Trump is speaking, says the US holds all the cards, Iran has been defeated 'militarily' The major US stock indices close at record levels At a glance: US-Iran ceasefire extension tentatively agreed but awaiting Trump sign-off; nuclear language sticking points remain; Iran's claim of shooting down a US aircraft near Bushehr denied by US authorities Tokyo core CPI missed across all measures in May, extending a six-month slowdown; core ex-food and energy at 1.6% vs 1.9% forecast; Japanese factory output surprised to the upside in April RBNZ Governor Anna Breman signalled rates will rise sooner and by more than previously indicated Russian drone struck a residential building in Galați, Romania; NATO response expected to be diplomatic rather than kinetic Dell shares surged 25%+ after blowout first-quarter earnings; Pentagon's $9.7 billion software contract an additional driver SpaceX targeting $1.8 trillion IPO valuation; Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket exploded during a hot-fire test Japan MOF intervention data due 1000 GMT / 0600 Eastern covering April 28 to May 27 A tentative agreement to extend the US-Iran ceasefire for 60 days lifted risk sentiment through the session, but the deal remained unsigned and fragile. Vice President JD Vance said President Trump had yet to endorse the memorandum of understanding, with negotiations still grinding over language on Iran's nuclear programme, specifically the disposition of Tehran's highly enriched uranium stockpile and its future enrichment rights. Vance said he believed Iran was negotiating in good faith but could not guarantee an agreement would be reached. Iranian state television claimed a US aircraft had been destroyed near Bushehr, a report US authorities flatly denied. In Japan, Tokyo's key inflation gauges missed forecasts across the board in May. Core CPI, excluding fresh food, rose 1.3% year-on-year against a 1.5% forecast, marking a sixth consecutive month of slowdown. The core-core index, stripping out both fresh food and energy, came in at 1.6% against an expected 1.9%. Government subsidies on utilities and tuition were the primary drag, with analysts expecting inflation to re-accelerate as energy and import price pressures build. Offsetting the soft inflation data, separate figures showed Japanese factory output rose 0.8% in April, confounding forecasts for a 0.9% decline, with manufacturers projecting a further 5.1% rise in May. The soft data complicates the BOJ's messaging ahead of its June policy meeting, where overnight index swaps had been pricing around an 80% probability of a hike to 1% from the current 0.75%. With subsidies distorting the headline reads, the BOJ's case for June rests heavily on the expectation that inflation will re-accelerate once those effects drop out. Japan's TOPIX moved to a record high. New Zealand dominated the central bank narrative. RBNZ Governor Anna Breman, whose casting vote broke a 3-3 split on the monetary policy committee to keep the OCR on hold at 2.25% this week, made clear the pause is temporary. Breman said the OCR is likely to increase sooner and by more than previously signalled, with the bank focused on returning inflation to target while managing economic volatility. Assistant Governor Karen Silk had earlier reinforced the hawkish message, saying the bank does not need to wait for a quarterly CPI print to act and that the bias is toward rate increases at coming meetings. The New Zealand dollar firmed on the guidance. Currencies otherwise consolidated against a broadly flat US dollar. Traders nudged away from the greenback on ceasefire optimism but stopped well short of a full risk-on move, with the deal's unsigned status keeping positioning cautious. A Russian one-way attack drone entered Romanian airspace during a strike on Ukraine and struck a residential apartment block in the eastern city of Galați, injuring several people. Romania's defence ministry confirmed the incident and scrambled F-16s. NATO's response is expected to be diplomatic, likely involving the summoning of the Russian ambassador and increased economic pressure rather than any kinetic reply. Dell shares surged more than 25% after the company reported its strongest earnings since returning to public markets in 2018. Revenue hit a record $43.8 billion, up 88% year-on-year, while diluted EPS rose 282% to $5.24. The Pentagon's award of a five-year, $9.7 billion software purchasing agreement to Dell Federal Systems added further impetus, reinforcing the market's view of Dell as a major AI infrastructure supplier rather than a traditional hardware company. Separately, SpaceX is targeting a valuation of at least $1.8 trillion in its forthcoming IPO, while Jeff Bezos' Blue Origin reported an anomaly during a hot-fire test of its New Glenn rocket, which exploded in footage circulated on social media. Japan's Ministry of Finance intervention data covering the period from April 28 to May 27 is due at 1000 GMT, 0600 Eastern. Bloomberg analysis of BOJ accounts had pointed to up to ¥10 trillion deployed in late April and through Golden Week, and the official figure will set the tone for yen trading heading into the weekend. This article was written by Eamonn Sheridan at investinglive.com.

items.title: investingLive Americas FX news wrap 28 May: Softer PCE pressures dollar and yields
items.summary: The major US stock indices close at record levels Reports that Iran targeted 4 American ships that attempted to cross the Straits of Hormuz Crude Oil futures settle up $0.22 at $88.90. Technical bias is to the downside. Treasury Secretary Bessent: Oil prices will be lower than pre-conflict levels More from Musalem: Bond market is signaling a resilient economy/higher expected inflation. US Treasury sells $77B of 7 year notes at a high yield of 4.290% US/Iran MOU on ceasefire has reached an agreement but Trump still needs to approve it Crude oil inventories fall -3.327 million versus -4.143 million estimate Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate for Q2 comes in at 3.8% down from 4.3% More from Fed's Musalem: Baseline is inflation will take longer to come down Feds Musalem: Caution warranted in the face of upward inflation pressures Axios: US and Iran have reached a tentative 60-day Memorandum of Understanding US new home sales for April 0.622 million versus 0.665 million estimate More from Feds Williams: We have a solid US economy. Feds Williams: Higher productivity rates can raise real interest rates US April durable goods orders +7.9% vs +3.5% expected US Core PCE for April YoY 3.3% vs 3.3% estimate. MoM 0.2% vs 0.3% estimate US initial jobless claims 215K vs 211K expected investingLive European markets wrap: Oil up, stocks down on continued US-Iran tensions The dollar softened broadly today, pressured by slightly cooler-than-expected PCE data which provided some relief from higher rates along with optimism around a proposed Memorandum of Understanding to kick off advanced peace talks. Both developments added to the headwinds of safe-haven demand for the greenback. Below is the list of the currencies which advanced the most vs the USD New Zealand Dollar – +0.54% Canadian Dollar +0.44% Swiss Franc +0.37% Australian Dollar – +0.32% Euro – +0.22% Japanese Yen +0.16% British Pound – +0.10% The commodity-linked Aussie, Canadian Dollar and Kiwi led gains, likely buoyed by the risk-on tone from the peace talk developments. The Euro and Sterling also firmed modestly. In the US debt market today, Treasury yields moved lower across the curve, with the longer-end leading the decline. The 10-year yield fell 3.2 basis points while the 30-year yield dropped 3.4 basis points, reflecting stronger demand for longer-duration debt and a more favorable tone in the bond market. The move lower in yields also came as softer inflation concerns and easing geopolitical tensions helped support buying in Treasurys. 7-year auction: The U.S. Treasury’s 7-year note auction was met with solid demand from international buyers, helping keep downward pressure on yields. Fed officials were less hopeful about inflation although uncertainty continues to persist. New York Fed President John Williams struck a balanced tone, leaning slightly hawkish overall as he emphasized the importance of keeping inflation expectations anchored and reiterated the Fed’s commitment to returning inflation to the 2% target. Williams acknowledged the U.S. economy and labor market remain solid, while highlighting rising productivity trends supported in part by AI and broader U.S. economic dynamism. However, he warned that supply-chain disruptions tied to the war could push inflation higher in the near term, with headline inflation expected to peak near 4% and core inflation around 3% in coming months. Despite those concerns, he said longer-term inflation expectations remain stable and stressed that monetary policy is currently “well-positioned.” Williams repeated that future policy decisions will remain data dependent, signaling the Fed is not ready to commit to either easing or tightening until the inflation and growth outlook becomes clearer. St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem also struck a cautious and somewhat hawkish tone in remarks today, emphasizing that inflation risks remain tilted to the upside even as the Fed keeps policy steady. Musalem said the Fed’s real policy rate is now below the long-run neutral rate, while longer-term inflation expectations have started to drift higher and the labor market remains stable. Against that backdrop, he argued that policymakers need to maintain a “vigilant focus” on returning inflation to the Fed’s 2% target and warned that the process is likely to take longer than previously hoped. He added that if disinflation does not resume over the next one to two quarters, it would become a growing concern, particularly if inflation expectations continue to rise. Musalem also pushed back against the idea that stronger productivity gains from artificial intelligence will quickly solve the inflation problem. While acknowledging AI could eventually improve productivity, he said current evidence remains inconclusive and that relying on future productivity gains to offset present inflation pressures would be risky. He reiterated that the possibility of another rate increase is “greater than zero,” noting there is a scenario where the economy may require tighter policy if inflation remains sticky. At the same time, he acknowledged a separate path where growth slows later this year and inflation eases, which could open the door to rate cuts. For now, however, Musalem said risks remain skewed more toward inflation than toward weaker growth. He also noted that recent moves higher in bond yields largely reflect expectations for a higher neutral rate and said he supported removing the Fed’s prior “easing bias.” Overall, the comments leaned hawkish, with Musalem signaling the Fed is not yet confident inflation is moving sustainably back toward target. Fundamentally, the April PCE inflation report offered a mixed but slightly encouraging picture on inflation, with monthly price pressures coming in a bit softer than expected while annual readings remained sticky. Core PCE, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge excluding food and energy, rose 0.2% for the month versus 0.3% expected, while the year-over-year rate came in at 3.3%, matching estimates but ticking up from 3.2% previously. Headline PCE also came in a touch softer on the monthly basis at 0.4% versus 0.5% expected, while the annual rate held at 3.8% as forecast. More encouraging for the Fed, services inflation excluding energy and housing slowed to 0.1% from 0.3% last month, while broader core measures excluding food, energy, and housing rose 0.2%, also cooler than the prior month. On the consumer side, however, the report showed signs of softening demand and increasing financial strain. Personal income was unchanged in April versus expectations for a 0.4% rise, while personal spending increased just 0.1%. Real PCE rose only 0.1%, suggesting consumers are becoming more cautious as higher prices and borrowing costs weigh on budgets. The personal savings rate fell to just 2.6%, a historically low level that highlights how much consumers have drawn down pandemic-era savings. Historically, U.S. savings rates have averaged closer to 5%–8%, making the current reading one of the weakest in decades and closer to levels seen ahead of the 2008 financial crisis. The initial market reaction saw Treasury yields move modestly lower as traders focused on the softer monthly inflation readings, although the year-over-year inflation data still remains well above the Fed’s 2% target. Overall, the report suggests inflation progress is continuing, but only gradually, while cracks are beginning to emerge in consumer finances and spending trends. U.S. durable goods orders surged 7.9% in April, well above the 3.5% expected increase, driven largely by a sharp rise in transportation equipment orders, particularly aircraft bookings. The gain followed an upwardly revised 1.3% increase in March, signaling continued strength in headline manufacturing demand. Excluding transportation, orders still rose a solid 1.1%, beating the 0.5% estimate, while orders excluding defense jumped 8.1% after a decline the prior month. Despite the strong headline numbers, the report had a softer underlying tone for business investment. Non-defense capital goods orders excluding aircraft — the closely watched core capex component that feeds into GDP calculations — fell 1.1% versus expectations for a 0.4% increase. That decline followed a strong upwardly revised 3.9% gain in March, suggesting some payback after prior strength rather than a collapse in investment demand. Overall, the report points to resilient manufacturing activity on the surface, boosted by large transportation orders, but more moderate momentum underneath in core business spending. Markets often look beyond the volatile aircraft-driven headline number and focus more closely on the softer core capital goods data as a better gauge of future business investment trends. This article was written by Greg Michalowski at investinglive.com.
items.summaryZh: 美国主要股指收于历史高位。有报道称，伊朗袭击了4艘试图穿越霍尔木兹海峡的美国船只。原油期货收盘上涨0.22美元，报88.90美元。技术面呈下行趋势。财政部长贝森特表示：油价将低于冲突前的水平。穆萨莱姆补充道：债券市场正释放出经济韧性增强及通胀预期升高的信号。美国财政部以4.290%的高收益率售出770亿美元7年期国债 美国与伊朗就停火谅解备忘录达成协议，但仍需特朗普批准 原油库存减少332.7万桶，低于预期的414.3万桶 亚特兰大联储GDPNow对第二季度的预估为3.8%，低于之前的4.3% 美联储穆萨莱姆补充道： 基本预期是通胀回落将需要更长时间 美联储的穆萨莱姆：面对通胀上行压力，应保持谨慎 Axios：美国与伊朗已达成一份为期60天的初步谅解备忘录 美国4月新屋销售量为62.2万套，低于预期的66.5万套 美联储威廉姆斯进一步表示：美国经济基础稳固。 美联储威廉姆斯：生产率提高可能推高实际利率 美国4月耐用品订单环比增长7.9%，高于预期值3.5% 美国4月核心个人消费支出（PCE）同比上涨3.3%，与预期值持平。环比0.2%，低于预期的0.3% 美国初请失业金人数21.5万，高于预期的21.1万 investingLive欧洲市场综述：美伊紧张局势持续，油价上涨，股市下跌 受略低于预期的PCE数据影响，美元今日普遍走软，该数据缓解了市场对加息的担忧，同时市场对拟议中的谅解备忘录以启动高级别和平谈判持乐观态度。这两项进展都加剧了美元避险需求的逆风。以下是对美元涨幅最大的货币列表：新西兰元 – +0.54% 加元 +0.44% 瑞士法郎 +0.37% 澳元 – +0.32% 欧元 – +0.22% 日元 +0.16% 英镑 – +0.10%与大宗商品挂钩的澳元、加元和纽元领涨，这可能得益于和平谈判进展带来的风险偏好情绪。欧元和英镑也小幅走强。在今日的美国债市，国债收益率曲线各期限普遍走低，长期端领跌。 10年期国债收益率下跌3.2个基点，30年期国债收益率下跌3.4个基点，这反映出市场对长期债券的需求增强，且债券市场情绪更为乐观。收益率的下行还得益于通胀担忧缓解以及地缘政治紧张局势缓和，这些因素共同支撑了美国国债的买盘。7年期国债拍卖：美国财政部7年期国债拍卖受到国际买家的强劲需求支撑，有助于抑制收益率的下行压力。尽管不确定性依然存在，但美联储官员对通胀前景的乐观程度有所减弱。纽约联储主席约翰·威廉姆斯（John Williams）的表态较为平衡，整体略显鹰派，他强调了保持通胀预期稳定的重要性，并重申了美联储将通胀率降至2%目标的承诺。威廉姆斯承认美国经济和劳动力市场依然稳健，同时强调了生产率上升的趋势——这在一定程度上得益于人工智能以及美国经济的整体活力。但他警告称，与战争相关的供应链中断可能在短期内推高通胀，预计未来数月整体通胀率将升至4%左右，核心通胀率则在3%左右。 尽管存在这些担忧，他表示长期通胀预期依然稳定，并强调当前货币政策“定位恰当”。威廉姆斯重申，未来的政策决定仍将取决于数据，这表明在通胀和经济增长前景更加明朗之前，美联储尚不准备承诺采取宽松或紧缩政策。圣路易斯联储主席阿尔贝托·穆萨莱姆在今天的讲话中也采取了谨慎且略带鹰派的基调，强调即使美联储保持政策稳定，通胀风险仍偏向上行。穆萨莱姆表示，美联储的实际政策利率目前低于长期中性利率，而长期通胀预期已开始走高，劳动力市场依然稳定。在此背景下，他主张决策者需对将通胀率降至美联储2%的目标保持“高度警惕”，并警告称这一过程可能比此前预期的更漫长。他补充道，如果未来一到两个季度通胀率未能回落，这将成为日益严重的担忧，特别是如果通胀预期持续上升的话。 穆萨勒姆还反驳了“人工智能带来的更强劲生产率提升将迅速解决通胀问题”这一观点。尽管他承认人工智能最终可能提高生产率，但他表示目前的证据尚不充分，且依赖未来的生产率提升来抵消当前的通胀压力存在风险。他重申，再次加息的可能性“大于零”，并指出如果通胀持续顽固，经济可能需要更紧缩的政策。与此同时，他也承认另一种可能的走向：今年晚些时候经济增长放缓，通胀压力随之缓解，这可能为降息打开大门。不过，穆萨莱姆表示，目前风险仍更多地偏向通胀而非经济增长放缓。他还指出，近期债券收益率的上升在很大程度上反映了对更高中性利率的预期，并表示他支持取消美联储此前持有的“宽松倾向”。总体而言，这些言论倾向于鹰派，穆萨莱姆暗示美联储尚不确定通胀能否可持续地回归目标水平。 从基本面来看，4月份的个人消费支出（PCE）通胀报告呈现出喜忧参半但略显积极的通胀态势：月度价格压力略低于预期，而年度数据则依然居高不下。美联储偏好的通胀指标——剔除食品和能源的核心PCE——当月环比上涨0.2%，低于预期的0.3%；同比涨幅为3.3%，符合预期，但较此前3.2%的水平有所上升。整体PCE月环比涨幅也略有放缓，为0.4%，低于预期的0.5%，而年率则如预期般维持在3.8%。对美联储而言更令人鼓舞的是，剔除能源和住房的服务业通胀从上月的0.3%放缓至0.1%，而更广泛的核心指标（剔除食品、能源和住房）上涨0.2%，同样低于上月水平。然而，从消费者角度来看，报告显示出需求疲软和财务压力加大的迹象。4月个人收入持平，低于预期0.4%的增幅，而个人支出仅增长0.1%。实际个人消费支出（PCE）仅增长0.1%，表明随着物价上涨和借贷成本增加对家庭预算造成压力，消费者正变得更加谨慎。 个人储蓄率降至仅2.6%，这一历史低位凸显了消费者在疫情期间消耗了多少积蓄。从历史上看，美国储蓄率平均值通常在5%至8%之间，因此当前读数是数十年来最疲软的之一，且更接近2008年金融危机前的水平。市场初步反应是国债收益率小幅走低，交易员关注的是月度通胀数据走软，尽管同比通胀数据仍远高于美联储2%的目标。总体而言，该报告表明通胀仍在持续，但仅是渐进式，而消费者财务状况和消费趋势中已开始出现裂痕。4月美国耐用品订单激增7.9%，远超预期3.5%的增幅，这主要得益于运输设备订单的急剧上升，尤其是飞机订单。这一增长紧随3月经上修后1.3%的增幅之后，表明整体制造业需求依然强劲。剔除运输设备后，订单仍稳健增长1.1%，超出0.5%的预期；而剔除国防订单后，该数据在经历上月下滑后猛增8.1%。 尽管整体数据表现强劲，但报告中关于企业投资的基调却较为温和。剔除飞机在内的非国防类资本 goods 订单——这一备受关注的核心资本支出指标是计算 GDP 的重要组成部分——环比下降 1.1%，而市场预期为增长 0.4%。此前 3 月份该数据曾被大幅上修至增长 3.9%，此次下滑表明此前强劲表现后的回调，而非投资需求的崩溃。总体而言，该报告表面上显示制造业活动韧性十足，这得益于大型运输设备订单的提振，但核心企业支出的增长势头实际上较为温和。市场往往会忽略受飞机订单影响而波动剧烈的总数据，转而更密切关注增长放缓的核心资本 goods 数据，将其视为衡量未来企业投资趋势的更佳指标。本文作者为 investinglive.com 的 Greg Michalowski。
items.summaryEn: The major US stock indices close at record levels Reports that Iran targeted 4 American ships that attempted to cross the Straits of Hormuz Crude Oil futures settle up $0.22 at $88.90. Technical bias is to the downside. Treasury Secretary Bessent: Oil prices will be lower than pre-conflict levels More from Musalem: Bond market is signaling a resilient economy/higher expected inflation. US Treasury sells $77B of 7 year notes at a high yield of 4.290% US/Iran MOU on ceasefire has reached an agreement but Trump still needs to approve it Crude oil inventories fall -3.327 million versus -4.143 million estimate Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate for Q2 comes in at 3.8% down from 4.3% More from Fed's Musalem: Baseline is inflation will take longer to come down Feds Musalem: Caution warranted in the face of upward inflation pressures Axios: US and Iran have reached a tentative 60-day Memorandum of Understanding US new home sales for April 0.622 million versus 0.665 million estimate More from Feds Williams: We have a solid US economy. Feds Williams: Higher productivity rates can raise real interest rates US April durable goods orders +7.9% vs +3.5% expected US Core PCE for April YoY 3.3% vs 3.3% estimate. MoM 0.2% vs 0.3% estimate US initial jobless claims 215K vs 211K expected investingLive European markets wrap: Oil up, stocks down on continued US-Iran tensions The dollar softened broadly today, pressured by slightly cooler-than-expected PCE data which provided some relief from higher rates along with optimism around a proposed Memorandum of Understanding to kick off advanced peace talks. Both developments added to the headwinds of safe-haven demand for the greenback. Below is the list of the currencies which advanced the most vs the USD New Zealand Dollar – +0.54% Canadian Dollar +0.44% Swiss Franc +0.37% Australian Dollar – +0.32% Euro – +0.22% Japanese Yen +0.16% British Pound – +0.10% The commodity-linked Aussie, Canadian Dollar and Kiwi led gains, likely buoyed by the risk-on tone from the peace talk developments. The Euro and Sterling also firmed modestly. In the US debt market today, Treasury yields moved lower across the curve, with the longer-end leading the decline. The 10-year yield fell 3.2 basis points while the 30-year yield dropped 3.4 basis points, reflecting stronger demand for longer-duration debt and a more favorable tone in the bond market. The move lower in yields also came as softer inflation concerns and easing geopolitical tensions helped support buying in Treasurys. 7-year auction: The U.S. Treasury’s 7-year note auction was met with solid demand from international buyers, helping keep downward pressure on yields. Fed officials were less hopeful about inflation although uncertainty continues to persist. New York Fed President John Williams struck a balanced tone, leaning slightly hawkish overall as he emphasized the importance of keeping inflation expectations anchored and reiterated the Fed’s commitment to returning inflation to the 2% target. Williams acknowledged the U.S. economy and labor market remain solid, while highlighting rising productivity trends supported in part by AI and broader U.S. economic dynamism. However, he warned that supply-chain disruptions tied to the war could push inflation higher in the near term, with headline inflation expected to peak near 4% and core inflation around 3% in coming months. Despite those concerns, he said longer-term inflation expectations remain stable and stressed that monetary policy is currently “well-positioned.” Williams repeated that future policy decisions will remain data dependent, signaling the Fed is not ready to commit to either easing or tightening until the inflation and growth outlook becomes clearer. St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem also struck a cautious and somewhat hawkish tone in remarks today, emphasizing that inflation risks remain tilted to the upside even as the Fed keeps policy steady. Musalem said the Fed’s real policy rate is now below the long-run neutral rate, while longer-term inflation expectations have started to drift higher and the labor market remains stable. Against that backdrop, he argued that policymakers need to maintain a “vigilant focus” on returning inflation to the Fed’s 2% target and warned that the process is likely to take longer than previously hoped. He added that if disinflation does not resume over the next one to two quarters, it would become a growing concern, particularly if inflation expectations continue to rise. Musalem also pushed back against the idea that stronger productivity gains from artificial intelligence will quickly solve the inflation problem. While acknowledging AI could eventually improve productivity, he said current evidence remains inconclusive and that relying on future productivity gains to offset present inflation pressures would be risky. He reiterated that the possibility of another rate increase is “greater than zero,” noting there is a scenario where the economy may require tighter policy if inflation remains sticky. At the same time, he acknowledged a separate path where growth slows later this year and inflation eases, which could open the door to rate cuts. For now, however, Musalem said risks remain skewed more toward inflation than toward weaker growth. He also noted that recent moves higher in bond yields largely reflect expectations for a higher neutral rate and said he supported removing the Fed’s prior “easing bias.” Overall, the comments leaned hawkish, with Musalem signaling the Fed is not yet confident inflation is moving sustainably back toward target. Fundamentally, the April PCE inflation report offered a mixed but slightly encouraging picture on inflation, with monthly price pressures coming in a bit softer than expected while annual readings remained sticky. Core PCE, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge excluding food and energy, rose 0.2% for the month versus 0.3% expected, while the year-over-year rate came in at 3.3%, matching estimates but ticking up from 3.2% previously. Headline PCE also came in a touch softer on the monthly basis at 0.4% versus 0.5% expected, while the annual rate held at 3.8% as forecast. More encouraging for the Fed, services inflation excluding energy and housing slowed to 0.1% from 0.3% last month, while broader core measures excluding food, energy, and housing rose 0.2%, also cooler than the prior month. On the consumer side, however, the report showed signs of softening demand and increasing financial strain. Personal income was unchanged in April versus expectations for a 0.4% rise, while personal spending increased just 0.1%. Real PCE rose only 0.1%, suggesting consumers are becoming more cautious as higher prices and borrowing costs weigh on budgets. The personal savings rate fell to just 2.6%, a historically low level that highlights how much consumers have drawn down pandemic-era savings. Historically, U.S. savings rates have averaged closer to 5%–8%, making the current reading one of the weakest in decades and closer to levels seen ahead of the 2008 financial crisis. The initial market reaction saw Treasury yields move modestly lower as traders focused on the softer monthly inflation readings, although the year-over-year inflation data still remains well above the Fed’s 2% target. Overall, the report suggests inflation progress is continuing, but only gradually, while cracks are beginning to emerge in consumer finances and spending trends. U.S. durable goods orders surged 7.9% in April, well above the 3.5% expected increase, driven largely by a sharp rise in transportation equipment orders, particularly aircraft bookings. The gain followed an upwardly revised 1.3% increase in March, signaling continued strength in headline manufacturing demand. Excluding transportation, orders still rose a solid 1.1%, beating the 0.5% estimate, while orders excluding defense jumped 8.1% after a decline the prior month. Despite the strong headline numbers, the report had a softer underlying tone for business investment. Non-defense capital goods orders excluding aircraft — the closely watched core capex component that feeds into GDP calculations — fell 1.1% versus expectations for a 0.4% increase. That decline followed a strong upwardly revised 3.9% gain in March, suggesting some payback after prior strength rather than a collapse in investment demand. Overall, the report points to resilient manufacturing activity on the surface, boosted by large transportation orders, but more moderate momentum underneath in core business spending. Markets often look beyond the volatile aircraft-driven headline number and focus more closely on the softer core capital goods data as a better gauge of future business investment trends. This article was written by Greg Michalowski at investinglive.com.

items.title: US Core PCE for April YoY 3.3% vs 3.3% estimate. MoM 0.2% vs 0.3% estimate
items.summary: Prior month YoY Core PCE 3.2%, headline 3.5% Core PCE YoY 3.3% vs 3.3 est. Core PCE MoM 0.2% vs 0.3% est Headline PCE YoY 3.8% vs 3.8% est Headline PCE MoM 0.4% vs 0.5% est. PCE Ex Food, energy and housing for April 0.2% vs 0.3% last month PCE services. Ex Energy and housing for April 0.1% vs 0.3% last month Real PCE increased $18.1 billion or 0.1% at a monthly rate) in April. The Personal income and Personal consumption data for April Personal Income MoM for April 0.0% vs 0.4% estimate. Prior 0.6% revised to 0.5% Personal consumption for April 0.1% vs 0.3% last month (revised from 0.2%. Personal saving was $611.7 billion in April, and the personal saving rate—personal saving as a percentage of DPI—was 2.6 percent. The decrease in current-dollar personal income in April primarily reflected a decrease in farm proprietors’ income that was partly offset by an increase in compensation. The PCE data came in a little bit lower than expected on a month-to-month.. The year on year data came in as expected. US yields have moved lower with the 10 year up 0.6 basis points or 4.486%. The two year yield is up 1.4 basis points at 4.047%.. A problem is the savings rate at 2.6%. The current U.S. personal savings rate of 2.6% is historically very low. Over the long term, the average U.S. savings rate has typically ranged between 5% and 8%. Before the pandemic, savings rates generally fluctuated near 6%–7%. During the COVID period, the savings rate surged to record highs above 30% as stimulus payments, reduced spending opportunities, and government support boosted household cash balances. Since then, consumers have steadily drawn down those excess savings as inflation and higher living costs pressured household budgets. A 2.6% rate is closer to the lows seen before the 2008 financial crisis and ranks among the weakest readings of the last several decades. US stocks are still lower but off their lowest levels with the S&P down -1.36 points. He down thus far is -65 points, and the NASDAQ index down -27 points implied by the futures. The USD has moved lower. The EURUSD have moved above the 100/200 hour MA at 1.1623 and 1.16287. The high price reached 1.1634 so far. Staying above 1.1623 is the close risk now for buyers. The USDJPY has moved to a new low at 159.34. On the downside, the 100 hour moving average at 159.19 and the 200 hour moving average at 159.086 are the next downside targets that if broken would increase the bearish bias from a technical perspective. On Tuesday, the low price installed against the rising 200 hour moving average increasing their levels of importance going forward. The GBPUSD has moved up to retest the 200 day moving average of 1.34203. Just above that is the 200 hour moving average is at 1.34315. The high price of just reach 1.3422 and currently trades at 1.3419. The 50% midpoint of the move up from the March low comes in at 1.3408 and is close risk for buyers looking for more upside momentum. Stay above is more bullish. What is the PCE and why is it so important? The U.S. PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures) Price Index is one of the Federal Reserve’s preferred measures of inflation because it tracks how much consumers are paying for goods and services across the economy and adjusts for changes in consumer behavior. The report is released monthly by the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) and includes both headline inflation and the closely watched “core” measure, which excludes food and energy prices because they tend to be more volatile. The PCE report measures price changes across a broad range of categories including housing, healthcare, transportation, food, energy, and services. Unlike CPI, the PCE index allows for substitutions when consumers shift spending patterns — for example, buying cheaper alternatives when prices rise — making it generally viewed as a more flexible and comprehensive inflation gauge. The Fed’s long-term inflation target of 2% is based on the Core PCE Price Index, making the report especially important for interest rate expectations, bond yields, the U.S. dollar, and stock markets. In addition to inflation data, the report also includes: Personal income Consumer spending Savings rate As a result, markets use the report not only to monitor inflation pressures, but also to gauge the strength of the U.S. consumer and the broader economy. In addition to the above the US 2nd revision to Q1 GDP was also released in the US. Prior estimate 2.0%. Q4 0.7% GDP 1Q +1.6% versus 2.0% expected Sales Preliminary 1.5% vs 1.7% estimate. Prior estimate 1.6% GDP consumer spending preliminary 1.4% versus 1.6% prior Deflatior 3.5% versus 3.6% preliminary Core PCE Q1 4.5% versus 4.5% preliminary PCE Q1 ex Food, energy and housing 4.7% versus 4.6% preliminary. Prior quarter to 20% PCE Q1 Services. Ex Energy and Housing 4.6% versus 4.4% preliminary. Prior quarter 3.6% The Q1 GDP is in the rearview mirror and not the main focus. Markets are more focused on the PCE, Durable goods, etc. This article was written by Greg Michalowski at investinglive.com.
items.summaryZh: 上月同比核心个人消费支出（PCE）3.2%，整体PCE 3.5%；核心PCE同比3.3%，符合3.3%的预期；核心PCE环比0.2%，低于0.3%的预期；整体PCE同比3.8%，符合3.8%的预期；整体PCE环比0.4%，低于0.5%的预期。4月剔除食品、能源和住房的PCE环比增长0.2%，低于上月的0.3%；4月剔除能源和住房的PCE服务类环比增长0.1%，低于上月的0.3%；4月实际PCE环比增长181亿美元，按月率计算增长0.1%。4月个人收入和个人消费数据：4月个人收入环比0.0%，低于0.4%的预期。此前0.6%的数据修正为0.5%；4月个人消费0.1%，低于上月的0.3%（从0.2%修正而来）。4月个人储蓄额为6117亿美元，个人储蓄率（即个人储蓄占可支配个人收入的百分比）为2.6%。4月按当前美元计算的个人收入下降，主要反映了农场主收入的减少，这一下降部分被薪酬的增加所抵消。PCE数据环比略低于预期。同比数据符合预期。美国国债收益率有所下行，10年期国债收益率上升0.6个基点至4.486%。2年期国债收益率上升1.4个基点至4.047%。一个问题是储蓄率仅为2.6%。当前美国 2.6%的个人储蓄率在历史上处于极低水平。从长期来看，美国的平均储蓄率通常在5%至8%之间。在疫情爆发前，储蓄率一般在6%至7%左右波动。在新冠疫情期间，随着刺激支票发放、消费机会减少以及政府支持措施的实施，家庭现金余额大幅增加，储蓄率飙升至超过30%的历史高位。此后，随着通胀和生活成本上升给家庭预算带来压力，消费者逐渐消耗了这些过剩储蓄。2.6%的储蓄率更接近2008年金融危机前的低点，也是近几十年来最疲软的读数之一。美股仍呈下跌态势，但已脱离低点，标普500指数下跌1.36点。期货数据显示，目前跌幅为65点，纳斯达克指数下跌27点。美元汇率走低。欧元兑美元已升至1.1623和1.16287的100/200小时均线之上。盘中最高触及1.1634。目前对多头而言，能否守住1.1623上方是关键风险点。美元兑日元已跌至159.34的新低。 在下方，159.19的100小时移动平均线和159.086的200小时移动平均线是接下来的下行目标，若被跌破，从技术角度来看将加剧看跌倾向。周二，汇价在上升的200小时移动平均线附近创下低点，这使得该均线在未来走势中的重要性进一步提升。英镑兑美元已回升至1.34203的200日移动平均线附近进行重新测试。该水平上方不远处便是位于1.34315的200小时移动平均线。盘中高点刚触及1.3422，目前交投于1.3419。自3月低点以来的这波上涨行情的50%回撤位位于1.3408，对于寻求更多上行动能的买方而言，该水平构成近期的风险。若能维持在该水平上方，则看涨信号更强。什么是PCE，它为何如此重要？美国个人消费支出（PCE）价格指数是美联储首选的通胀衡量指标之一，因为它追踪了消费者在整个经济体中为商品和服务支付的费用，并针对消费者行为的变化进行了调整。 该报告由经济分析局（BEA）每月发布，其中既包含整体通胀率，也包含备受关注的“核心”通胀指标——后者排除了食品和能源价格，因为这两类价格通常波动较大。个人消费支出（PCE）报告衡量了包括住房、医疗保健、交通、食品、能源和服务在内的广泛类别中的价格变动。与CPI不同，PCE指数允许消费者在改变消费模式时进行替代——例如，当价格上涨时购买更便宜的替代品——因此通常被视为一种更灵活、更全面的通胀指标。美联储2%的长期通胀目标是基于核心PCE价格指数制定的，这使得该报告对利率预期、债券收益率、美元汇率和股市尤为重要。除通胀数据外，该报告还包含：个人收入、消费者支出、储蓄率。因此，市场不仅利用该报告监测通胀压力，还借此评估美国消费者及整体经济的韧性。此外，美国还发布了第一季度国内生产总值（GDP）的第二次修正值。此前预估为2.0%。 第四季度GDP增长0.7%，第一季度增长1.6%，低于预期值2.0%；零售额初步数据为1.5%，低于1.7%的预估。此前预估：GDP 1.6%，消费者支出初步值1.4%，低于此前1.6%的预估；平减指数3.5%，低于此前3.6%的初步值；核心PCE第一季度4.5%，与此前4.5%的初步值持平；剔除食品、能源和住房的PCE第一季度4.7%，高于此前4.6%的初步值。上季度至20%的PCE第一季度服务业。剔除能源和住房后的PCE为4.6%，而初步值为4.4%。上一季度为3.6%。第一季度GDP数据已成过去，并非市场关注重点。市场更关注PCE、耐用品等指标。本文由investinglive.com的Greg Michalowski撰写。

Prior 0.6% revised to 0.5% Personal consumption for April 0.1% vs 0.3% last month (revised from 0.2%. Personal saving was $611.7 billion in April, and the personal saving rate—personal saving as a percentage of DPI—was 2.6 percent. The decrease in current-dollar personal income in April primarily reflected a decrease in farm proprietors’ income that was partly offset by an increase in compensation. The PCE data came in a little bit lower than expected on a month-to-month.. The year on year data came in as expected. US yields have moved lower with the 10 year up 0.6 basis points or 4.486%. The two year yield is up 1.4 basis points at 4.047%.. A problem is the savings rate at 2.6%. The current U.S. personal savings rate of 2.6% is historically very low. Over the long term, the average U.S. savings rate has typically ranged between 5% and 8%. Before the pandemic, savings rates generally fluctuated near 6%–7%. During the COVID period, the savings rate surged to record highs above 30% as stimulus payments, reduced spending opportunities, and government support boosted household cash balances. Since then, consumers have steadily drawn down those excess savings as inflation and higher living costs pressured household budgets. A 2.6% rate is closer to the lows seen before the 2008 financial crisis and ranks among the weakest readings of the last several decades. US stocks are still lower but off their lowest levels with the S&P down -1.36 points. He down thus far is -65 points, and the NASDAQ index down -27 points implied by the futures. The USD has moved lower. The EURUSD have moved above the 100/200 hour MA at 1.1623 and 1.16287. The high price reached 1.1634 so far. Staying above 1.1623 is the close risk now for buyers. The USDJPY has moved to a new low at 159.34. On the downside, the 100 hour moving average at 159.19 and the 200 hour moving average at 159.086 are the next downside targets that if broken would increase the bearish bias from a technical perspective. On Tuesday, the low price installed against the rising 200 hour moving average increasing their levels of importance going forward. The GBPUSD has moved up to retest the 200 day moving average of 1.34203. Just above that is the 200 hour moving average is at 1.34315. The high price of just reach 1.3422 and currently trades at 1.3419. The 50% midpoint of the move up from the March low comes in at 1.3408 and is close risk for buyers looking for more upside momentum. Stay above is more bullish. What is the PCE and why is it so important? The U.S. PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures) Price Index is one of the Federal Reserve’s preferred measures of inflation because it tracks how much consumers are paying for goods and services across the economy and adjusts for changes in consumer behavior. The report is released monthly by the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) and includes both headline inflation and the closely watched “core” measure, which excludes food and energy prices because they tend to be more volatile. The PCE report measures price changes across a broad range of categories including housing, healthcare, transportation, food, energy, and services. Unlike CPI, the PCE index allows for substitutions when consumers shift spending patterns — for example, buying cheaper alternatives when prices rise — making it generally viewed as a more flexible and comprehensive inflation gauge. The Fed’s long-term inflation target of 2% is based on the Core PCE Price Index, making the report especially important for interest rate expectations, bond yields, the U.S. dollar, and stock markets. In addition to inflation data, the report also includes: Personal income Consumer spending Savings rate As a result, markets use the report not only to monitor inflation pressures, but also to gauge the strength of the U.S. consumer and the broader economy.
